source.title: Three Catastrophic Hydrogen Fluoride Incidents at Honeywell Geismar

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Hydrogen Fluoride Release
event.incident_type: Multiple incidents: gasket failure with fatality (Oct 2021), reboiler explosion (Jan 2023), HF release with serious injury (Jun 2024)
actions_taken: Medical treatment provided to injured personnel
Manual valve closure to stop leak
Third-party audit of process safety systems initiated
Safer Technologies and Alternatives Analysis (STAA) performed
event.summary: Three separate hydrogen fluoride incidents occurred at Honeywell Geismar's HFC-245fa unit between October 2021 and June 2024. The first incident (October 21, 2021) involved catastrophic failure of a spiral wound gasket in a flanged piping connection, releasing 39 pounds of anhydrous HF and fatally injuring Field Operator 2 who was exposed to the toxic vapor. The second incident (January 23, 2023) involved catastrophic failure of a reboiler that had thinned due to HF corrosion, releasing over 800 pounds of anhydrous HF and over 1,600 pounds of chlorine gas; no personnel were injured but a complex-wide shelter-in-place was initiated. The third incident (June 7, 2024) involved partial removal of a blind flange from a valve containing pressurized HF during maintenance, releasing less than one pound of HF and seriously injuring a contract worker. All three incidents were preventable through effective implementation of existing safety management systems.
recommendations: Perform comprehensive third-party audit of Geismar facility's process safety and allied management systems evaluating compliance with federal standards and internal management systems (2023-02-I-LA-R1)
Require periodic reporting updates from Geismar site regarding closure of audit findings (2023-02-I-LA-R2)
Perform Safer Technologies and Alternatives Analysis (STAA) for HFC-245fa unit (2023-02-I-LA-R3)
EPA to initiate prioritization of hydrogen fluoride as High-Priority Substance under TSCA and conduct risk evaluation (2023-02-I-LA-R4)
OSHA to amend PSM standard to require MOC review for organizational changes impacting process safety (reiterated 2005-04-I-TX-R9)
key_phrases: management of organizational and personnel change

threats.name: Spiral wound gasket stress corrosion cracking and deterioration
Flange assembly fastener corrosion and loosening
Reboiler shell thinning due to HF corrosion
Inadequate HF removal from piping prior to maintenance
Delayed implementation of gasket technology change
Loss of critical process knowledge due to personnel turnover
Backflow of HF through failed check valve
Startup of equipment not ready for operation

consequences.name: Fatal exposure to anhydrous hydrogen fluoride vapor
Serious injury from HF exposure (facial and respiratory burns)
Catastrophic reboiler explosion and vapor cloud release
Off-site impact - shelter-in-place order and highway closure
Property damage and loss of production

controls.name: Mechanical Integrity Program - Piping Inspection
controls.barrier_role: Detection of corrosion damage to gaskets and flange assemblies
controls.lod_basis: Scheduled inspections to identify deterioration before failure

supporting_text: Honeywell inspected the piping segment containing the incident gasket twice within the 36 days prior to the incident. Neither inspection identified any issues with the gasket or flange assembly which Honeywell later concluded were likely already damaged by corrosion at the time of both inspections.

Honeywell also did not use available technologies that could have helped its inspectors or operators detect either corrosion damage to the incident flange assembly or the HF leak prior to the incident. Such technologies included HF atmospheric monitors and acid indicating paint, both of which are strongly recommended by industry guidance documents and by Honeywell itself.

controls.name: Technology Change Implementation - Gasket Material Upgrade
controls.barrier_role: Prevention of gasket stress corrosion cracking through material selection
controls.lod_basis: Replacement of susceptible spiral wound gaskets with corrosion-resistant technology identified in 2007

supporting_text: Within the first five years of the 245 unit's operation, Honeywell identified corrosion problems with spiral wound gaskets in certain HF services in the unit. Fourteen years prior to the 2021 incident, in 2007, Honeywell identified a new gasket technology and conducted a Management of Change (MOC) analysis, but elected to replace the gaskets within the unit by attrition because the company believed the existing gaskets were appropriate for the process. By 2021, Honeywell had not yet fully implemented the technology change when one of these spiral wound gaskets failed catastrophically and fatally injured a Honeywell operator.

controls.name: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) - Level D for Routine Operations
controls.barrier_role: Protection of personnel from HF exposure during routine operations
controls.lod_basis: Minimum PPE level for routine operator rounds and non-emergency tasks

supporting_text: Field Operators 1 and 2 were both wearing Level D PPE (Figure 4), the least protective level prescribed by Honeywell.

Neither of the two field operators were wearing adequately protective PPE, and when the gasket in the flange assembly failed, Field Operator 2 was exposed to the released toxic HF and was fatally injured.
His personal protective equipment (PPE) was not sufficient to protect against either skin or respiratory exposure to HF.

controls.name: Safe Work Practices - Unit Startup Procedures
controls.barrier_role: Ensure equipment is ready for operation before startup attempts
controls.lod_basis: Written operating procedures to verify system readiness prior to startup

supporting_text: On the day of the incident, Honeywell attempted to start up equipment that was not ready for operation. Honeywell's startup attempt, which did not follow its written operating procedures, resulted in a no-flow condition within the unit.
Honeywell did not follow its written unit startup procedures, which caused the fatally injured operator to be in the immediate vicinity of the incident gasket.

controls.name: Mechanical Integrity Program - Reboiler Inspection and Replacement
controls.barrier_role: Detection and replacement of corroded reboiler shell before catastrophic failure
controls.lod_basis: Scheduled thickness measurements and capital project to replace shell when minimum thickness approached

supporting_text: Honeywell Geismar ran the incident reboiler to failure four times since commissioning the unit in 2002. Leading up to the 2023 failure, Honeywell inspected the reboiler in October 2021, identified that the shell was nearing its minimum thickness, and initiated a capital project to procure a replacement shell. Honeywell never purchased the replacement shell, however, and the reboiler ran to catastrophic failure in January 2023.
The reboiler had thinned over time due to corrosion and the failure occurred under otherwise normal operating conditions.

controls.name: Management of Organizational Change (MOOC)
controls.barrier_role: Ensure critical safety tasks are reassigned when key personnel depart
controls.lod_basis: Formal procedure to account for and reassign safety-critical responsibilities during organizational changes

supporting_text: Only one employee at the Geismar site had working knowledge of the reboiler's condition—the 245 unit maintenance engineer, who also had responsibility for the capital project to replace the reboiler shell. After this engineer left the company in April 2022, Honeywell never reassigned the capital project to another employee. Honeywell had an existing, applicable management system entitled 'Management of Organizational Change' (MOOC) that should have ensured that the maintenance engineer's key safety tasks, including the reboiler project, were accounted for and reassigned, but Honeywell did not apply the MOOC procedure in this case.

controls.name: Capital Project Management - Reboiler Shell Replacement Funding
controls.barrier_role: Ensure prioritized safety-critical capital projects are funded and completed
controls.lod_basis: Capital budgeting process to allocate resources to high-priority safety projects

supporting_text: Honeywell had two primary philosophies for managing capital expenditure requests and projects. Larger, more complex, and more expensive projects were handled through the company's annual and forward-looking capital expenditure budgeting process. Less expensive, less complex, though still important projects were managed and funded through what was supposed to be a faster and less administratively complex process. The project to replace the reboiler shell was approved for the faster management path and should have been funded shortly thereafter, but it was never funded. At the same time, however, the site chose to fund other projects that were either unprioritized or rated with lower priority than the reboiler shell replacement.

controls.name: Organizational Resilience - Safety Management System Continuity
controls.barrier_role: Maintain effective safety management systems during periods of operational disruption
controls.lod_basis: Policies and procedures to ensure safety systems remain effective despite operational challenges

supporting_text: After the October 2021 fatal incident, several adverse conditions combined to cause significant disruption to Honeywell Geismar's normal business and safety management systems. After that incident, the site focused on returning the 245 unit to safe operation. According to Honeywell personnel, this effort consumed nearly all the site's attention, manpower, and resources for several months following the October 2021 incident. Honeywell's approach to addressing the causes of the 2021 incident may have caused it to miss the conditions, disruptions, and safety management system implementation gaps that led to the reboiler explosion.

controls.name: Safe Work Practices - Equipment Preparation for Maintenance
controls.barrier_role: Ensure all hazardous materials are removed from equipment before maintenance work begins
controls.lod_basis: Procedures to verify complete removal of HF from piping and equipment prior to opening for maintenance

supporting_text: Honeywell did not adequately prepare the HF piping for maintenance, leaving a small amount of HF trapped between the incident valve plug and blind flange.
One of the workers opened a flanged connection that, unknown to the worker or to Honeywell, was not adequately emptied of HF prior to the start of the work.

controls.name: Hazard Identification and Mitigation - Closed Valve Control
controls.barrier_role: Identify and control hazards from closed valves that may contain pressurized HF
controls.lod_basis: Work procedures to identify and isolate closed valves that may contain hazardous materials

supporting_text: In the field, neither Honeywell nor personnel who worked for Honeywell's contractor, Turner Industries Group ('Turner'), identified or resolved the hazard of the closed and therefore potentially HF containing valve.

controls.name: HF Atmospheric Monitoring
controls.barrier_role: Detection of HF vapor leaks in the work environment
controls.lod_basis: Continuous or periodic monitoring to detect HF vapor presence before personnel exposure

supporting_text: Honeywell also did not use available technologies that could have helped its inspectors or operators detect either corrosion damage to the incident flange assembly or the HF leak prior to the incident. Such technologies included HF atmospheric monitors and acid indicating paint, both of which are strongly recommended by industry guidance documents and by Honeywell itself.

controls.name: Acid Indicating Paint
controls.barrier_role: Visual detection of HF acid corrosion on equipment surfaces
controls.lod_basis: Paint coating that changes color in presence of HF acid to indicate corrosion

supporting_text: Such technologies included HF atmospheric monitors and acid indicating paint, both of which are strongly recommended by industry guidance documents and by Honeywell itself.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
